cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~30.0; measured 29.3 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 22-year-old presents with sudden shortness of breath, tingling fingers, and chest tightness during emotional distress.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.2; measured 21.5 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (142 − (106 + 10) = 26) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 67-year-old presents with dizziness and volume depletion after recurrent nausea and retching.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~47.0; measured 46.3 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of high-output gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter’s formula predicts PaCO2 ~36.5 (±2); measured 36.1 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 − (110 + 19) = 10, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and perioral tingling after an acute stressful event.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.4; measured 21.4 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter’s formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (±2); measured 36.2 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 142 − (116 + 18) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 32-year-old is brought in drowsy with bradypnoea and decreased responsiveness.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.8; measured 26.4 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with reduced consciousness, shallow respirations, and pinpoint pupils.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.7; measured 26.1 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 28-year-old presents unwell with dehydration, tachypnoea, and reduced oral intake over 2 days.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter’s formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (±2); measured 27.2 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 − (102 + 13) = 23 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.
